q: In dim light, what happens to the pupil?
The process of focusing on objects at different distances is called what?
When focusing on a distant object, what is the state of the ciliary muscles?
When the eyes focus on a nearby object, what happens to the lens?
Which structure holds the lens vertically in the eye and assists it to change thickness?
Which part of the eye provides the clearest vision and contains only cone cells?
Which fluid maintains the internal pressure of the eye and provides nutrients for the lens and cornea?
What is the role of the iris?
a: To control the amount of light entering the eye

q: In bright light, which muscles of the iris contract to constrict the pupil?
What is the advantage of having two eyes that allows a person to estimate the speed of moving objects more accurately?
Which part of the eye acts as a screen for the formation of images?
Which structure refracts light rays onto the retina and can change its focal length?
The ciliary muscles relax and the pressure in the eye pushes outwards on the lens when the eye is in what state?
a: At rest, focusing on long distance

q: When focusing on a nearby object, what happens to the suspensory ligaments?
What is the function of the optic nerve?
a: Transmits nerve impulses from the retina to the brain
